docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Plain-English meanings of the words and equipment names used in this documentation. If a term in another page is unfamiliar, look it up here.

docs.title: Equipment names
docs.text:
Allen & Heath QU-5D : The audio mixer — the large desk with sliders. It balances all sound and creates the room, foyer and livestream mixes. See QU-5D Mixer.

JBL SRX812P : The powered speakers in the main auditorium. "Powered" means each speaker has its own amplifier built in.

Foyer amplifier : The amplifier that drives the foyer speakers, fed by its own mix from the QU-5D.

RodeCaster Video : The video switcher and livestream encoder. Combines the cameras with the livestream audio and streams to YouTube. See RodeCaster Video.

Camera 1 — RoboShot HDMI 12 : The first PTZ camera, usually the wide shot.

Camera 2 — AVKANS 20X PTZ Camera Pro : The second PTZ camera, usually close-ups; has a 20× zoom.

AVKANS Joy Pro Controller : The joystick box used to move the cameras by hand. See PTZ Controller.

StreamDeck XL : The panel of labelled buttons on the desk. Each button runs a job (switch camera, start stream, change slide). See StreamDeck Buttons.

Bitfocus Companion : The software (on the NUC) that makes the StreamDeck buttons work.

Presentation PC (NUC) : The small Windows computer that runs PowerPoint and Companion.

Bluestream HDMI Matrix : The box that decides which picture shows on which screen. See Bluestream Matrix.

RTI 7" touch screen controller : The small touch screen just below the PC used to turn the TVs on and off. It controls TV power only — the picture comes from the Bluestream matrix. See TV Distribution.

Rode M5 : A pair of wired condenser microphones used for the choir and organ.

Sennheiser condenser microphone : The wired microphones at the lectern (Ch 1) and pulpit (Ch 5).

docs.title: Audio terms
docs.text:
Mixer : The desk that combines and balances all the microphones and sends sound to the speakers, foyer and livestream.

Fader : A sliding volume control for one source. Up = louder, down = quieter.

Mute : A button that silences a channel even if its fader is up. Lit = silenced.

Channel : One input strip on the mixer, e.g. "Channel 1 = lectern mic".

Mix : A particular balance of sources for one destination. We have three: room, foyer and livestream.

Matrix mix : A separate, independently-balanced mix. The foyer and livestream each use a matrix mix, which is why a mic can be in one mix but not another. See Livestream Mix.

Main / LR fader : The fader controlling the overall level to the church speakers.

Feedback : The loud squeal when an open mic hears the speakers and loops. Fix by lowering that mic's fader.

Condenser microphone : A sensitive wired microphone (no battery needed) — our lectern, pulpit and Rode M5 mics.

Radio (wireless) microphone : A battery-powered wireless microphone — our two handhelds and the headset.

docs.title: Video terms
docs.text:
PTZ : Pan, Tilt, Zoom — a motorised camera that can turn and zoom on command.

Pan : Turning the camera left/right.

Tilt : Tipping the camera up/down.

Zoom : Making the picture closer (in) or wider (out).

Preset : A saved camera position (pan/tilt/zoom) you can recall with one button.

Switching : Choosing which camera the livestream shows. It does not move the cameras.

Source : An input — e.g. a camera or the PC — that can be shown or sent somewhere.

Encoder : A device that packages video for streaming. The RodeCaster Video does this for YouTube.

Livestream : The live broadcast of the service to YouTube.

docs.title: Display terms
docs.text:
HDMI : The common cable/connector that carries picture and sound between devices and TVs.

Matrix : A switch that can route any input to any output — our Bluestream matrix sends the PC picture to the chosen screens.

Confidence monitor : A screen facing the speaker so they can see the slides without turning around.

Input / Output : An input is a source coming in (e.g. the PC). An output is a screen going out (e.g. Front TV Left).

No Signal : A TV message meaning it is not receiving a picture — usually wrong input or routing. See TV Not Working.

docs.title: General terms
docs.text:
Slide Show mode : PowerPoint showing slides full screen (press F5), with no menus.

Scene (QU-5D) : A saved snapshot of all mixer settings — recall it to return to the normal Sunday setup. See QU-5D Scenes.

Power-cycle : To turn a device off, wait, and on again — a common fix.

Mills IT : The company that maintains this system. Contact details on Regular Checks.

docs.title: Volunteer Training
docs.text:
This page is a training path for a brand-new volunteer operator. Work through it with an experienced operator over a few Sundays. By the end you will be able to run a service confidently on your own.

You do not need to be technical

Everything is done with a small number of labelled buttons and faders. Take it one step at a time. It is fine to ask questions and to keep this guide open while you operate.

docs.title: What an operator does (in one paragraph)
docs.text: Each Sunday you turn the system on, open the right microphones as people speak, switch between two cameras and advance the slides, run the YouTube livestream, deal with any small problems, and then shut the system down safely. That's it.

docs.title: Training stage 1 — Watch and learn
docs.text:
Sit with an experienced operator for one or two full services. Just watch and ask questions. Notice:

  • The order things are turned on.
  • When microphones are opened and closed.
  • When the operator switches cameras and changes slides.
  • How the livestream is started and stopped.

Read alongside: Sunday Startup and Running a Service.

docs.title: Training stage 2 — Do the slides and cameras
docs.text:
With a mentor beside you, take over the easy, low-risk jobs:

  • Advance the PowerPoint slides → PowerPoint Operation.
  • Switch cameras with the StreamDeck → Camera Operation.

These are forgiving — a slightly late slide or a wide shot held a little long is never a problem.

docs.title: Training stage 3 — Run the sound
docs.text:
Now learn the microphones:

  • Which mic is on which channel → Microphone Guide.
  • Opening and closing faders as people speak.
  • Checking radio mic batteries → Battery Replacement.
  • Avoiding feedback.

Read: QU-5D Mixer.

docs.title: Training stage 4 — Run the livestream
docs.text:
Learn the streaming jobs:

  • Start and stop the stream → RodeCaster Video.
  • Confirm picture and sound on YouTube.
  • Understand the livestream mix is separate from the room → Livestream Mix.

docs.title: Training stage 5 — Startup, shutdown and problems
docs.text:
Finally, learn the bookends and the safety net:

  • Full Sunday Startup on your own.
  • Full Sunday Shutdown on your own.
  • The Troubleshooting pages — know where to look, you don't need to memorise them.

docs.title: The golden rules
docs.text:
Remember these five

  1. Mixer on before speakers; speakers off before mixer. (Avoids pops.)
  2. Sound in the room comes first. Livestream and slides are second.
  3. Only open microphones that are in use. (Avoids noise and feedback.)
  4. Move the off-air camera, then switch to it. (No on-screen swinging.)
  5. Stop the stream before shutting anything down.

docs.title: Confidence checklist
docs.text:
You're ready to operate solo when you can, without help:

  • [ ] Start the whole system in the right order.
  • [ ] Identify each microphone and open/close it on the QU-5D.
  • [ ] Switch cameras and recall a camera preset.
  • [ ] Advance and black the PowerPoint slides.
  • [ ] Start the livestream and confirm picture + sound on YouTube.
  • [ ] Find the right troubleshooting page for a common problem.
  • [ ] Shut the system down safely and put batteries on charge.

docs.title: When you're unsure
docs.text:
• Keep this site open on a phone or tablet at the desk.
  • Use Search (top of the page) to jump straight to an answer.
  • For anything you can't solve during a service, keep the in-room service going and note it for Mills IT.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Glossary — plain-English meaning of every term
  • Running a Service
  • Troubleshooting

docs.title: Troubleshooting: Camera Not Moving
docs.text:
Use this page when a camera won't pan, tilt or zoom — the joystick or a StreamDeck preset button does nothing, or the camera won't go to a saved position.

Most common cause

The wrong camera is selected on the controller, so the joystick is moving a camera you're not watching.

docs.title: Step-by-step checks
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Camera won't move] --> B{Using the joystick or a StreamDeck preset?}
    B -- Joystick --> C{Is the correct camera selected on the controller?}
    C -- No --> C1[Press the Camera 1 or Camera 2 select button]
    C -- Yes --> D{Is the controller powered/connected?}
    D -- No --> D1[Check controller power and network cable]
    D -- Yes --> E[Power-cycle the camera; note for Mills IT]
    B -- Preset --> F{Do StreamDeck buttons light up?}
    F -- No --> F1[NUC/Companion off - see StreamDeck Buttons]
    F -- Yes --> G[Camera moved/renamed; use joystick instead]

docs.title: A. The joystick won't move the camera
docs.text:
1. Right camera selected? On the AVKANS Joy Pro Controller, press the Camera 1 or Camera 2 select button for the camera you want to move. The joystick only moves the selected camera.
  2. Are you watching the camera you're moving? You may be moving the off-air camera correctly — switch the livestream/preview to it to confirm.
  3. Controller powered and connected? Check the controller has power and its network/control cable is connected.
  4. Camera responsive at all? If still nothing, power-cycle the camera (off, wait ~20 seconds, on) and try again.

➡️ Controller detail: PTZ Controller.

docs.title: B. A StreamDeck preset button won't move the camera
docs.text:
1. Do the StreamDeck buttons light up at all? If no, the NUC or Companion is off — the buttons can't work. See StreamDeck Buttons.
  2. Buttons light up but the preset does nothing? The camera may have been moved or its preset changed. Use the joystick to aim it for now and note it for Mills IT.

docs.title: C. The camera moves but the wrong way / too fast
docs.text:
This is normal joystick behaviour, not a fault:

  • Push the joystick gently for slow movement, further for faster.
  • If it moves the opposite way to what you expect, simply move it the other direction. Make small movements.

docs.title: Keep the service going
docs.text:
A still camera is fine

If a camera won't move, just leave it on a sensible wide shot and run the service from there. You do not need camera movement to livestream successfully.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Fix Joystick does nothing Wrong camera selected Press the correct camera-select button Joystick still does nothing Controller not connected Check controller power/cable Preset button does nothing, buttons unlit NUC/Companion off Turn on NUC; restart Companion Preset wrong position Preset changed/camera moved Use joystick; note for Mills IT

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• PTZ Controller
  • Camera Operation
  • StreamDeck Buttons
  • No Camera Video

docs.title: Troubleshooting: No Camera Video
docs.text:
Use this page when a camera shows no picture — for example the RodeCaster shows black where a camera should be, or the livestream picture is black or frozen.

Most common cause

A camera is switched off / not finished starting up, or the wrong source is selected on the RodeCaster.

docs.title: Which camera?
docs.text: Camera Model Usual shot Camera 1 RoboShot HDMI 12 Wide Camera 2 AVKANS 20X PTZ Camera Pro Close-up

docs.title: Step-by-step checks
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Camera shows no picture] --> B{Is the camera powered on?}
    B -- No --> B1[Turn the camera on, wait for it to start]
    B -- Yes --> C{Does the RodeCaster preview show that camera?}
    C -- No --> C1[Check HDMI cable / camera input on RodeCaster]
    C -- Yes --> D{Is that camera selected as the live source?}
    D -- No --> D1[Press the Camera 1 or Camera 2 button]
    D -- Yes --> E[Restart the camera; if still black, note for Mills IT]
  1. Is the camera on? Check the camera has power and its indicator is lit. Cameras take a little time to start — wait ~30 seconds after power on.
  2. Does the RodeCaster preview show it? Look at the RodeCaster Video screen. If that camera's preview is black:
    • The HDMI cable from the camera may be loose — check both ends if safe to do so.
    • The camera may still be starting up — wait, then try again.
  3. Is it selected as the live source? If the preview is fine but the livestream is black, you may simply be showing a different/blank source. Press the Camera 1 or Camera 2 button to select it. See Camera Operation.
  4. Still black? Restart that camera (power off, wait, power on). If it still has no picture, switch the livestream to the other camera and note the fault for Mills IT.

docs.title: Picture is frozen (not black)
docs.text:
• The camera or its connection may have glitched. Switch to the other camera, then power-cycle the frozen one (off, wait, on).
  • If the whole livestream picture froze, the issue may be the internet — see Network Overview.

docs.title: Keep the service going
docs.text:
One camera is enough

If one camera fails, run the whole livestream on the other camera — usually Camera 1 (wide). A single steady wide shot is perfectly acceptable. Do not let a camera fault stop the stream.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Fix Black where a camera should be Camera off / starting / HDMI loose Power on, wait, check cable Livestream black but preview fine No live source selected Press Camera 1 / Camera 2 Picture frozen Camera glitch or internet Switch cameras; power-cycle; check network Both cameras black Power or RodeCaster issue Check power; restart RodeCaster

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Camera Operation
  • RodeCaster Video
  • Camera Not Moving
  • Network Overview

docs.title: Troubleshooting: No Livestream Audio
docs.text:
Use this page when the picture is fine on YouTube but online viewers can't hear (or the sound is too quiet online). The room may sound perfectly fine — that is expected, because the livestream uses a separate audio mix.

Most common cause

A microphone is up for the room but is not in the livestream matrix mix, or the livestream matrix master is down/muted on the QU-5D.

docs.title: First, confirm the problem
docs.text:
1. On a phone, open the YouTube stream (use headphones to avoid feedback).
  2. Have someone speak into the microphone in question.
  3. If the room hears them but the stream does not → it's a livestream mix problem (this page). If the room can't hear them either → go to No Sound first.

docs.title: Step-by-step checks
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[No sound on YouTube] --> B{Does the room hear the mic?}
    B -- No --> B1[Fix room sound first - see No Sound] 
    B -- Yes --> C{RodeCaster audio meters moving?}
    C -- No --> C1[Livestream matrix master down/muted, or audio cable to RodeCaster] 
    C -- Yes --> D{Is the stream actually live and selected?}
    D -- No --> D1[Start the stream - see RodeCaster Video]
    D -- Yes --> E[Specific mic not in livestream matrix - raise its send]
  1. Room hears it? If not, fix the room first → No Sound.
  2. RodeCaster audio meters moving? Look at the RodeCaster Video audio meters. If they are not moving when someone speaks:
    • The livestream matrix master on the QU-5D may be down or muted — raise / un-mute it.
    • Or the audio cable from the QU-5D to the RodeCaster has a problem — note for Mills IT.
  3. Meters moving but still nothing on YouTube? Confirm the stream is actually live → RodeCaster Video.
  4. One specific mic missing online only? That microphone is not in the livestream matrix. Raise its send to the livestream matrix on the QU-5D → Livestream Mix.

docs.title: How the livestream audio is meant to flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    MIC[Microphone] --> QU[QU-5D]
    QU -->|Livestream Matrix Mix| RCV[RodeCaster Video]
    RCV --> YT[YouTube]

If any link in this chain is missing, online viewers lose sound while the room may be unaffected.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: What you see Likely cause Fix No sound online, room fine, meters still Livestream matrix master down/muted Raise / un-mute it on QU-5D No sound online, meters moving Stream not live, or YouTube delay Confirm stream is live; wait a few seconds One mic missing online only Mic not in livestream matrix Raise that mic's livestream send Audio crackly / cutting out Cable or connection Note for Mills IT

docs.title: If you can't fix it during the service
docs.text: The in-room service is unaffected — carry on. Note the time and symptom and contact Mills IT afterwards. Online viewers can be told (via a slide or the YouTube description) if audio was affected.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Livestream Mix
  • RodeCaster Video
  • No Sound
  • QU-5D Mixer

docs.title: Troubleshooting: No Sound
docs.text:
Use this page when there is no sound in the church (the main auditorium), or when one microphone can't be heard. Work through the checks in order and stop as soon as the sound comes back.

Stay calm — it's usually simple

The most common causes are a fader down, a mute button on, or a radio mic switched off / flat battery. Check those first.

docs.title: A. No sound at all in the room
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[No sound in the room] --> B{Are the JBL speakers powered on?}
    B -- No --> B1[Turn the speakers on] --> Z[Test again]
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the QU-5D mixer on?}
    C -- No --> C1[Turn the mixer on, then speakers] --> Z
    C -- Yes --> D{Is the main LR fader up?}
    D -- No --> D1[Raise the main LR fader to its mark] --> Z
    D -- Yes --> E{Is the speaking mic's fader up and un-muted?}
    E -- No --> E1[Raise the fader, press MUTE off] --> Z
    E -- Yes --> F[See section C below or call Mills IT]

Step by step:

  1. Speakers on? Check the JBL SRX812P speakers are powered. If off, turn them on.
  2. Mixer on? Check the QU-5D is on and the screen is lit. If off, turn the mixer on first, then the speakers.
  3. Main fader up? The main (LR) fader controls overall room volume. Raise it to its normal mark.
  4. Is the L/R button lit above the master fader? If it is not lit, the faders are controlling the foyer or livestream mix, not the room — press L/R so it lights up, then test the faders again.
  5. The mic in use — fader up and un-muted? Raise that channel's fader and make sure its MUTE light is off.

Moved a fader and nothing changed?

Two things to check on the mixer:

  • The L/R button above the master fader is lit — otherwise you are adjusting a different mix and the room volume will not respond. See Master / main fader.
  • The Layer A button (bottom left) is lit — otherwise the faders are on a different bank of channels, not the mics. See Layers.

➡️ Mixer controls: QU-5D Mixer.

docs.title: B. One particular microphone can't be heard
docs.text:
Microphone Channel Check these Lectern 1 Fader up? Mute off? Handheld 2 Fader up? Mute off? Mic switched on? Battery good? Handheld 3 Fader up? Mute off? Mic switched on? Battery good? Headset 4 Fader up? Mute off? Mic switched on? Battery good? Pulpit 5 Fader up? Mute off? Choir/Organ Rode M5 Faders up? Mute off?

For radio microphones (2, 3, 4) the extra checks matter most:

  1. Is the microphone switched on?
  2. Is the battery good? Replace it if low — see Battery Replacement.
  3. Is the person speaking close enough to the mic?

If ALL three radio mics are dead at once

The three radio microphone receivers are powered by the top power strip. If none of Channels 2, 3 and 4 work (even with good batteries), that strip is probably off — switch it on. See Sunday Startup — Step 7.

➡️ Which mic is which: Microphone Guide.

docs.title: C. Sound in the room but not online or not in the foyer
docs.text:
The room, the foyer and the livestream are separate mixes:

  • Foyer quiet → Foyer Mix.
  • Livestream / online quiet → No Livestream Audio.

This is normal behaviour, not a fault

A microphone can be up for the room but not in the foyer or livestream matrix. The fix is to raise it in the correct matrix mix, not the main fader.

docs.title: D. A loud squeal (feedback)
docs.text:
If you get a loud squeal:

  1. Lower the fader of the microphone causing it (usually the one just opened or held near a speaker).
  2. Keep handheld mics pointed at the mouth, away from speakers.
  3. Close any microphones not in use.

docs.title: Still no sound?
docs.text:
If you have worked through the steps and there is still no sound:

  • The service can continue using a handheld radio mic as a backup if the fixed mics fail, or vice-versa.
  • Note exactly what happened and contact Mills IT — see Regular Checks for contact details.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• QU-5D Mixer
  • Microphone Guide
  • No Livestream Audio
  • Battery Replacement

docs.title: Troubleshooting: PowerPoint Not Displaying
docs.text:
Use this page when the slides aren't showing correctly — the TVs show the PowerPoint editing view or the desktop instead of full-screen slides, or the slides won't advance.

Most common cause

PowerPoint is not in Slide Show mode. Press F5 on the presentation PC to start the full-screen slideshow.

docs.title: Step-by-step checks
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Slides not showing right] --> B{Is PowerPoint open with the correct file?}
    B -- No --> B1[Open this week's service file]
    B -- Yes --> C{Is it in Slide Show full-screen mode?}
    C -- No --> C1[Press F5 to start the slideshow]
    C -- Yes --> D{Do the front TVs show the slides?}
    D -- No --> D1[Matrix/TV issue - see TV Not Working]
    D -- Yes --> E{Will the slides advance?}
    E -- No --> E1[Click the slide area, then use arrow keys]
  1. Right file open? Make sure PowerPoint is open with this week's service file.
  2. Slide Show mode? Press F5 (start from beginning) or Shift + F5 (start from current slide) to go full screen.
  3. TVs not showing it? If the PC is in Slide Show mode but the TVs don't show slides, it's a screen/routing issue → TV Not Working and Bluestream Matrix.
  4. Slides won't advance? Click once on the slide area to make sure PowerPoint has "focus", then use the right arrow / spacebar.

docs.title: The TVs show the desktop or PowerPoint menus
docs.text:
This means PowerPoint is not presenting full screen. Press F5.

Presenter View vs Slide Show

If the PC shows notes/thumbnails while the TVs show the slide, that is Presenter View working correctly. If the TVs show the notes view, PowerPoint is showing on the wrong display — see the next section.

docs.title: Slides appear on the wrong screen
docs.text:
If the slideshow opens on the PC's own screen instead of the TVs:

  1. In PowerPoint, go to the Slide Show tab.
  2. Find Monitor and set it to the correct display (the one feeding the matrix / TVs).
  3. Restart the slideshow (F5).

You can also use the Windows shortcut {++Windows++ + ++P++} to choose how the displays are arranged (e.g. Duplicate or Extend).

Don't experiment during the service

If display settings get tangled mid-service, the quickest reliable fix is often to set PowerPoint to Duplicate so the TVs mirror the PC, get the slides showing, and sort out the proper Extend setup afterwards.

docs.title: Slides frozen or PowerPoint stuck
docs.text:
1. Press Esc to leave the slideshow.
  2. Restart the slideshow with F5 (or Shift + F5 from the current slide).
  3. If PowerPoint is fully frozen, close and reopen it; as a last resort restart the NUC (this also restarts Companion / the StreamDeck).

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Fix TVs show desktop/menus Not in Slide Show mode Press F5 Slides on PC not TVs Wrong monitor selected Slide Show → Monitor; or Slides won't advance PowerPoint lost focus Click slide, use arrow keys Everything frozen PowerPoint/PC stuck Esc, reopen, or restart NUC

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• PowerPoint Operation
  • TV Distribution
  • Bluestream Matrix
  • StreamDeck Buttons

docs.title: Troubleshooting: TV Not Working
docs.text:
Use this page when a TV or screen is blank, shows "No Signal", or shows the wrong picture.

Most common causes

The TV is on the wrong HDMI input, the matrix is routed wrong, or PowerPoint is not in full-screen Slide Show mode.

docs.title: Which screen?
docs.text: Screen Where Normally shows Front TV Left Front, left Slides Front TV Right Front, right Slides Rear Confidence Monitor Faces speaker Slides Fireplace TV Fireplace Slides (if used) Meeting Room TV Meeting room Slides (if used)

docs.title: Step-by-step checks
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[TV blank or wrong picture] --> B{Is the TV powered on?}
    B -- No --> B1[Turn the TV on via the RTI touch screen]
    B -- Yes --> C{Does it show No Signal?}
    C -- Yes --> D{Is the TV on the correct HDMI input?}
    D -- No --> D1[Use TV remote: Source/Input to correct HDMI]
    D -- Yes --> E{Is the Bluestream matrix routing the PC to this TV?}
    E -- No --> E1[Re-route output to PowerPoint PC]
    C -- No, shows desktop --> F[PowerPoint not full screen - press F5]
  1. TV on? Turn it on from the RTI 7" touch screen (just below the PC) — the TVs are powered from the RTI, not the rack power strip.
  2. "No Signal"? Then the TV isn't receiving a picture:
    • Wrong HDMI input: Use the TV remote's Source / Input button and select the correct HDMI input.
    • Matrix routing: Check the Bluestream matrix is sending the PowerPoint PC to this screen → Bluestream Matrix.
  3. Shows the desktop, not slides? PowerPoint isn't full-screen — press F5 on the PC → PowerPoint Not Displaying.
  4. Only one front TV out? That TV's power, its HDMI cable, or its input — the other TV working tells you the PC and matrix are fine.

docs.title: All TVs blank
docs.text:
If every screen is blank:

  • Check the TVs were turned on from the RTI touch screen (try "all on" again).
  • Check the Bluestream matrix has power.
  • Check the PowerPoint PC (NUC) is on and outputting a picture.
  • See TV Distribution and Bluestream Matrix.

docs.title: Keep the service going
docs.text:
The room can run without the TVs

If the TVs fail, the spoken service and singing continue. If song words are essential, a printed sheet or the band's own copies can cover the gap while you fix the screens or note them for Mills IT.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Fix One TV "No Signal" Wrong input or cable Set correct HDMI input; check cable TV shows desktop PowerPoint not full-screen Press F5 TV shows wrong source Matrix routed wrong Re-route to PowerPoint PC All TVs blank Matrix or PC issue Check matrix power and PC

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• TV Distribution
  • Bluestream Matrix
  • PowerPoint Not Displaying

docs.title: Camera Operation
docs.text: This page is about choosing and switching camera shots during the service — the everyday camera job. For moving a camera by hand with the joystick, see PTZ Controller.

docs.title: The two cameras
docs.text:
Camera 1 Camera 2 Model RoboShot HDMI 12 AVKANS 20X PTZ Camera Pro Usual shot Wide — the whole front Close-up — speaker / lectern Zoom Standard 20× (strong)

Camera 1 = wide, Camera 2 = close

When you are not sure which to use, choose Camera 1 (wide). A steady wide shot always looks acceptable on the livestream.

docs.title: What switching does
docs.text: "Switching" means choosing which camera the livestream shows. It does not move the cameras — it just picks which one is on screen. The cameras stay where they are pointed.

docs.title: How to switch which camera is shown
docs.text:
The easiest way is the StreamDeck:

  1. Press the Camera 1 button to show the wide shot.
  2. Press the Camera 2 button to show the close-up.

The picture changes smoothly. You can also switch on the RodeCaster Video itself.

➡️ Buttons explained: StreamDeck Buttons. ➡️ The switcher: RodeCaster Video.

docs.title: Camera presets (saved positions)
docs.text:
A preset is a camera position (pan, tilt and zoom) that has been saved in advance — for example "lectern close-up" or "wide front". Pressing a preset button sends the camera smoothly to that saved spot, so you do not have to aim it by hand.

To use a preset:

  1. Press the preset button on the StreamDeck (e.g. Cam 2 → Lectern).
  2. The camera moves itself to the saved position.

Presets are the easy way to get good shots

Most of a normal service can be run just by pressing camera switch and preset buttons on the StreamDeck. You only need the joystick for unusual shots.

docs.title: A simple shot plan for a normal service
docs.text:
During… Show Why Singing / whole congregation Camera 1 (wide) Captures the whole front and atmosphere Someone speaking at lectern Camera 2 close-up of lectern Viewers see the speaker clearly Preaching from the pulpit Camera 2 close-up of pulpit Focus on the preacher Music with choir/organ Camera 1 (wide) or a choir preset Shows the musicians Transitions / unsure Camera 1 (wide) Safe, always looks fine

Hold each shot for a few seconds

Avoid switching cameras too often. Let a shot settle for several seconds. Frequent switching is distracting for online viewers.

docs.title: Don't switch while moving
docs.text:
Switch to a still camera, not a moving one

Do not put a camera on the livestream while it is still moving to a new position — viewers will see it pan and zoom. Move Camera 2 while Camera 1 is being shown, then switch to Camera 2 once it has arrived.

Example: 1. Camera 1 (wide) is on the livestream. 2. Send Camera 2 to the "pulpit close-up" preset (it moves behind the scenes). 3. Once Camera 2 has arrived, switch the livestream to Camera 2.

docs.title: Video Overview
docs.text:
This section explains the cameras and the livestream — how we film the service and send it to YouTube. Like the audio section, it starts with the big picture and then gives step-by-step detail on each part.

If you only read one thing

The RodeCaster Video is the centre of the video system. The two cameras feed into it, the audio feeds into it, and it streams the finished picture to YouTube. You mostly control it through the StreamDeck buttons.

docs.title: The big picture
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    CAM1[Camera 1 - RoboShot HDMI 12] --> RCV[RodeCaster Video]
    CAM2[Camera 2 - AVKANS 20X PTZ] --> RCV
    AUD[Livestream Audio Mix from QU-5D] --> RCV
    RCV --> YT[YouTube Livestream]
    JOY[AVKANS Joy Pro Controller] -. moves .-> CAM1
    JOY -. moves .-> CAM2
    SD[StreamDeck + Companion] -. switches/presets .-> RCV
    SD -. recalls camera positions .-> CAM1
    SD -. recalls camera positions .-> CAM2

docs.title: The parts and what they do
docs.text:
Item What it is What you do with it Camera 1 — RoboShot HDMI 12 A motorised PTZ camera at the front Usually the wide shot Camera 2 — AVKANS 20X PTZ Camera Pro A motorised PTZ camera Usually close-ups (20× zoom) RodeCaster Video Video switcher + livestream encoder Choose which camera is shown; start/stop the stream AVKANS Joy Pro Controller A joystick box for the cameras Move and zoom the cameras by hand StreamDeck XL + Companion Labelled buttons that automate jobs Switch cameras, recall camera positions, start/stop stream

What 'PTZ' means

PTZ stands for Pan, Tilt, Zoom. These cameras have motors so they can turn left/right (pan), up/down (tilt) and zoom in/out — without anyone standing behind them.

docs.title: How you normally control the video
docs.text:
You have two ways to control the cameras:

  1. The StreamDeck (easiest) — press a button to switch to a camera or to send a camera to a saved position (a "preset"). This is what you will use most. See StreamDeck Buttons.
  2. The AVKANS Joy Pro Controller (manual) — a joystick to move a camera live by hand when you need a shot that isn't saved. See PTZ Controller.

To choose which camera the livestream shows, use the RodeCaster Video (usually via a StreamDeck button). See Camera Operation.

docs.title: Which camera is which
docs.text:
Camera 1 Camera 2 Model RoboShot HDMI 12 AVKANS 20X PTZ Camera Pro Typical use Wide shot of the front Close-ups (speaker, lectern) Zoom Standard 20× (strong zoom)

Remember it this way

Camera 1 = wide, Camera 2 = close. When unsure, stay on Camera 1 (the wide shot) — it always looks acceptable.

docs.title: PTZ Controller
docs.text:
The AVKANS Joy Pro Controller is the joystick box used to move the cameras by hand. Use it when you need a shot that has not been saved as a preset. For everyday switching and presets, the StreamDeck is easier.

📷 Screenshot placeholder: AVKANS Joy Pro Controller with joystick, zoom rocker and camera-select buttons labelled.

docs.title: The joystick
docs.text:
Moving the joystick pans and tilts the selected camera:

  • Push left / right → the camera turns left / right (pan).
  • Push up / down → the camera looks up / down (tilt).
  • Push gently → it moves slowly. Push further → it moves faster.

docs.title: The zoom control
docs.text:
A rocker or twist control zooms the selected camera:

  • One way zooms in (closer / tighter).
  • The other way zooms out (wider).

docs.title: The camera select buttons
docs.text: Buttons that choose which camera the joystick controls — Camera 1 or Camera 2. The joystick only moves the camera you have selected.

docs.title: The preset buttons
docs.text: Number buttons that save and recall camera positions (used together with a "set"/"store" button). These match the presets you can also reach from the StreamDeck.

docs.title: Before you move a camera
docs.text:
Only move a camera that is NOT live

Never move a camera while it is the one shown on the livestream — viewers will see it swing around. Switch the livestream to the other camera first, then move this one. See Camera Operation.

docs.title: How to move a camera by hand
docs.text:
1. Press the camera select button for the camera you want to move (Camera 1 or Camera 2).
  2. Confirm that camera is not currently on the livestream.
  3. Use the joystick to pan and tilt.
  4. Use the zoom control to frame the shot.
  5. When the shot looks good, switch the livestream to that camera (see Camera Operation).

Move gently

Small, slow movements look professional. Big, fast joystick moves look jerky on the livestream.

docs.title: How to move Camera 2 (worked example)
docs.text:
"How do I move Camera 2?"

  1. On the controller, press the Camera 2 select button.
  2. Make sure the livestream is currently showing Camera 1 (so viewers don't see Camera 2 move).
  3. Push the joystick to aim Camera 2 where you want.
  4. Use the zoom to get the framing right (Camera 2 zooms up to 20×).
  5. Switch the livestream to Camera 2 when ready.

docs.title: Saving a preset (optional, advanced)
docs.text:
If you have found a shot you will reuse, you can save it as a preset:

  1. Select the camera.
  2. Aim and zoom it exactly how you want.
  3. Hold/press the set (store) button, then a number button.

To recall it later, press that number button (or the matching StreamDeck preset button).

Check with Mills IT before changing existing presets

Recalling a preset is always safe. Overwriting an existing numbered preset changes it for everyone. If unsure, save to an unused number or ask Mills IT.

docs.title: RodeCaster Video
docs.text:
The RodeCaster Video is the box that runs the livestream. It takes the two cameras and the livestream audio, lets you choose which camera is shown, and sends the finished video to YouTube.

This page explains its main controls, then how to start and stop the livestream.

📷 Screenshot placeholder: RodeCaster Video front panel with key buttons labelled.

docs.title: The screen
docs.text: The RodeCaster Video has a touchscreen showing previews of the cameras and the current output (what viewers see).

docs.title: The source / scene buttons
docs.text: Buttons (and matching StreamDeck buttons) let you choose which camera is on the livestream — Camera 1, Camera 2, or a prepared scene.

docs.title: The Stream button
docs.text: This starts and stops sending the video to YouTube. When live, it shows a streaming indicator.

docs.title: The audio meters
docs.text: Small bars that move with the sound. They confirm the livestream audio (from the QU-5D) is arriving.

docs.title: How to start the livestream
docs.text:
Do this 2–3 minutes before the 9:30am service.

  1. Make sure the cameras are on and showing a picture on the RodeCaster screen.
  2. Make sure the audio meters move when someone speaks into a microphone.
  3. Press the Start Stream button on the StreamDeck (recommended), or the Stream button on the RodeCaster Video.
  4. Wait for the streaming indicator to confirm you are live.
  5. Open YouTube on a phone or the PC to confirm the stream appears with picture and sound.

Use the StreamDeck

The easiest and most reliable way to start the stream is the Start Stream button on the StreamDeck — it runs the correct steps for you. See StreamDeck Buttons.

Check audio before you go live

The most common livestream complaint is "no sound online". Always confirm the audio meters are moving before you start. See Livestream Mix.

docs.title: How to switch cameras during the stream
docs.text:
1. Press the Camera 1 or Camera 2 button (on the StreamDeck or RodeCaster).
  2. The livestream picture changes to that camera.

➡️ Full detail: Camera Operation.

docs.title: How to stop the livestream
docs.text:
At the end of the service:

  1. Keep streaming for a minute or two after the final words.
  2. Press the Stop Stream button on the StreamDeck (or the Stream button on the RodeCaster).
  3. Confirm the streaming indicator has switched off.

Always stop the stream before shutting down

Do not power equipment off while still live — viewers see an abrupt cut and YouTube may flag the broadcast. Stop the stream first. See Sunday Shutdown.

docs.title: If the stream won't start or drops out
docs.text:
Symptom First thing to check Page Stream won't start Cameras showing a picture? Internet connected? No Camera Video Picture but no sound online Audio meters moving? Livestream matrix up? No Livestream Audio Black / frozen picture online Camera source selected? Camera powered? No Camera Video Stream keeps disconnecting Internet connection Network Overview

The stream key lives in the RodeCaster

The RodeCaster Video is already set up with the church's YouTube stream settings. You should not need to enter a stream key. If YouTube reports the wrong account or channel, contact Mills IT — do not change the settings yourself.
